---
title: "Basic Transitive Resolution"
tagline: "Or what to Do When One Install Brings Three Packages"
type: scenario
tool: python
---

#### ! Scenario

*Or what to Do When One Install Brings Three Packages*
This scenario shows the ground truth every Python tool shares: installing one package pulls in its dependencies, and theirs, until the graph is satisfied. `demo-lib-a` needs `demo-lib-b`, which needs `demo-lib-c` — one command installs all three. It is the Python sibling of [MS1](../../maven-demo/scenario-1-basic/guide.qmd).

::: {.callout-note title="Tool Versions"}
Tested with **pip 25.3**, **Poetry 2.4**, **uv 0.11**. All three behave identically here; the differences begin in the next scenario.
:::

#### ! Example

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Your project] --> LibA[demo-lib-a 1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[demo-lib-b 1.0.0]
    LibB --> LibC[demo-lib-c 1.0.0]
```

```{.text include="pip/requirements.txt" snippet="p1-deps"}
```

#### ! Expect
Declaring only `demo-lib-a` installs `demo-lib-b` and `demo-lib-c` as well.

#### ! Why
Every tool resolves the graph transitively before installing. The one Python-specific rule to hold onto: the result lands in **one environment**, and that environment can hold only **one version** of each package. There is no classpath and no coexistence — a fact that turns the next scenario from an inconvenience into an impossibility.

#### ! Check

**Fast check (all three tools shown; pick yours):**

```bash
# pip
python -m venv .venv && . .venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt && pip list

# poetry
poetry lock && poetry install && poetry show --tree

# uv
uv sync && uv tree
```

**What to look for:** `demo-lib-c` appears in the installed set even though you never named it.

**Conclusion:** Transitive resolution is automatic and identical across tools.

#### ! Takeaway
One environment, one version per package. Everything else in this part follows from that single constraint.

#### ! Related
*   [P2](../scenario-2-conflict/guide.qmd) - What happens when two packages need different versions of the same thing.
*   [MS1](../../maven-demo/scenario-1-basic/guide.qmd) - The JVM equivalent (where multiple versions *can* coexist on a classpath).
*   [N1](../../node-demo/scenario-1-basic/guide.qmd) - The Node equivalent, where the tree holds as many versions as it needs.

#### ! Wild
Python's "one version per environment" rule is why the virtualenv exists at all. Before venvs were routine, installing two projects with incompatible dependencies into the system Python was a genuine way to break your operating system's own tooling — Debian and Red Hat both ship system utilities written in Python that share that global site-packages.
